claim: The 2021 systematic assessment estimated about 1.9 million stillbirths globally in 2019 and showed the highest stillbirth rates in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.

source.title: Global, regional, and national estimates and trends in stillbirths from 2000 to 2019: a systematic assessment

method: Reviewed Crossref metadata and the study title to anchor the stillbirth burden and trend estimate used for pack framing.
limitations: This record anchors burden framing but does not by itself provide sub-national operational thresholds.
The estimate is for 2019 and may not capture post-2019 disruption or more recent registration changes.

claim: The 2016 Lancet stillbirth series framed many late-gestation stillbirths as preventable and treated intrapartum stillbirth as a policy-relevant signal of failures in obstetric and newborn care.
method: Reviewed stable DOI metadata for the Lancet stillbirth series paper used to justify the pack's decision wedge around preventable intrapartum burden.
limitations: This source is a policy and evidence synthesis anchor, not direct proof about any current country or district.
Intervention tractability claims from 2016 need newer country-specific verification before operational use.

claim: The 2016 Lancet stillbirth series highlighted strong concentration of stillbirth burden in low-income settings and emphasized that rates, risk factors, and acceleration toward 2030 differ sharply by region and health-system capability.

source.title: Stillbirths: rates, risk factors, and acceleration towards 2030

method: Reviewed DOI metadata for the companion Lancet series article to support neglectedness and burden concentration framing.
limitations: This record does not by itself provide a current neglectedness funding denominator.
Regional framing does not solve sub-national comparability or public-data sufficiency.

claim: The DHS statistical guide defines the stillbirth rate as fetal deaths in pregnancies of at least 28 weeks or seven months divided by stillbirths plus live births, and documents a change from contraceptive-calendar to pregnancy-history inputs in DHS-8.
method: Reviewed the definition, numerator, denominator, DHS-7/DHS-8 calculation, and changes-over-time sections of the DHS statistical guide.
limitations: The guide describes survey-derived indicators and cannot establish national registration completeness or facility case ascertainment.
Reported pregnancy duration is respondent-reported and does not necessarily have a clinical gestational-age basis.
The indicator does not directly distinguish antepartum from intrapartum fetal death.

claim: The DHS-8 interviewer manual records pregnancy outcome, pregnancy duration, and a confirmation question distinguishing a stillbirth from a live birth that later died.
method: Reviewed the pregnancy-history instrument instructions and questions 216-217 for outcome coding and stillbirth confirmation.
limitations: An interviewer instrument does not measure whether respondents disclose every pregnancy outcome.
The manual cannot validate clinical gestational age, birthweight, or completeness of a survey sample.
The instrument does not provide facility-level intrapartum timing.

claim: WHO institutional-stillbirth metadata specifies facility numerator and denominator, 22-week and 28-week thresholds, and disaggregation by antepartum or intrapartum timing, gestational age, birthweight, and facility characteristics.
method: Reviewed the indicator definition, disaggregation, method of measurement, recommended international threshold, and preferred data-source sections.
limitations: Indicator metadata specify a measurement standard but do not prove that any country collects the fields completely.
Facility rates cover service-contact populations and can be distorted by referral, transfer, and case-mix differences.
The metadata do not establish that an observed rate difference is caused by care quality.

claim: UNICEF's HMIS data-quality guidance identifies vital status at birth, gestational age, and birthweight as core fields for classifying stillbirths and assessing whether routine data can support program decisions.

source.title: Stillbirth Definition and Data Quality Assessment for Health Management Information Systems

method: Reviewed the guidance overview and linked assessment document for required classification fields, data-quality uses, and intended users.
limitations: The guidance is an assessment framework and does not supply record-level data for a country or district.
It cannot by itself correct referral and denominator bias in institutional data.
Data-quality improvement does not prove that a stillbirth was preventable or that a facility performed poorly.

claim: WHO and UNICEF's 2021 MPDSR materials provide a facility and community workflow for identifying, notifying, reviewing, and responding to maternal and perinatal deaths, including stillbirths, with a blame-free confidentiality principle.

source.title: Maternal and perinatal death and surveillance and response: materials to support implementation

method: Reviewed the official overview and implementation purpose for population scope, review workflow, action linkage, confidentiality, and intended users.
limitations: Implementation guidance does not demonstrate complete case ascertainment in a named health system.
A review workflow cannot by itself quantify the national stillbirth burden.
Identifying modifiable factors is not equivalent to proving preventability for an individual death.

claim: WHO's CRVS assessment toolkit evaluates the legal framework, registration completeness, data flows, and quality checks needed before vital-registration counts are used for mortality analysis.

source.title: Improving the quality and use of birth, death and cause-of-death information: guidance for a standards-based review of country practices

method: Reviewed the official toolkit overview for system components assessed, field testing, and its intended use in prioritizing corrective action.
limitations: The toolkit is not a stillbirth prevalence dataset and cannot supply a country rate without a completed country assessment.
A system-level assessment does not resolve local stillbirth definition differences unless the legal and operational rules are separately documented.
It cannot distinguish intrapartum from antepartum burden without compatible event-level fields.
